desc: Largest LA robber fly. Catches prey in mid-air including dragonflies and grasshoppers. Bristly, powerful.

fm.Behavior: Aerial predator; catches prey in flight; perches on prominent twigs
fm.Diet: Predator — dragonflies, grasshoppers, bees, other large insects

desc: Large robber fly with bright orange-red legs. Named for eating other robber flies. Impressive size.

fm.Behavior: Aggressive aerial predator; sits on fence posts, bare branches
fm.Diet: Predator — other robber flies, bees, large insects

desc: Fuzzy hoverfly-like fly that feeds at flowers while hovering. Long proboscis. Larvae parasitize solitary bees.

fm.Color: Fuzzy golden-brown; long straight proboscis; dark wing front edge
fm.Behavior: Hovers at flowers; flicks eggs into bee burrows in flight
fm.Habitat: Gardens, chaparral, meadows — near ground-nesting bees
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize ground-nesting bee larvae

desc: Large, spectacular bee fly with mottled tiger-pattern wings. Parasitizes carpenter bees.

fm.Behavior: Hovers near carpenter bee nests; lays eggs in nest holes
fm.Habitat: Near wooden structures with carpenter bee holes
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize carpenter bee larvae

desc: Sinuous bee fly — dark bee fly with wavy dark wing markings. Larvae parasitize tachinid flies (hyperparasitoid). Common in chaparral. Hovers at flowers.
Largest NA fly — wingspan can exceed 3 in. Harmless despite mosquito-like appearance. Cannot bite.

fm.Color: Brown; very long legs; mosquito-like but MUCH larger
fm.Behavior: Attracted to lights; clumsy flight; adults do not feed; completely harmless
fm.Habitat: Near streams, moist areas — adults at lights
fm.Diet: Adults: do not feed (mouthparts non-functional). Larvae: aquatic detritus

desc: Large biting fly. Females require blood meal for eggs. Painful slashing bite. Found near livestock and water.

fm.Behavior: Females bite — slash skin, lap blood; painful; males visit flowers
fm.Diet: Females: blood. Males: nectar, pollen

desc: Bright orange abdomen; distinctive feathered hind legs. Parasitizes stink bugs and squash bugs. Beneficial.

fm.Color: Black thorax; bright orange abdomen; feathered hind tibiae
fm.Behavior: Parasitizes squash bugs and stink bugs; lays eggs on host
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize Hemiptera (beneficial)

desc: Metallic green-gold. First insect to colonize carrion — important in forensic entomology. Larvae used medically for wound cleaning.

fm.Behavior: First to arrive at carrion; important forensic indicator
fm.Habitat: Ubiquitous — attracted to carrion, garbage, wounds
fm.Diet: Larvae: carrion, decaying tissue

desc: Metallic blue. Large, buzzy. Common around garbage and pet waste. Distinctive loud buzzing.

fm.Behavior: Loud buzzer; attracted to meat, garbage
fm.Diet: Larvae: carrion, waste

desc: Looks like a house fly but BITES. Forward-pointing proboscis. Bites legs of humans and livestock.

fm.Color: Gray; like house fly but with bayonet-like proboscis (forward-pointing)
fm.Behavior: Painful biter; bites legs; rests on walls with head up; blood feeder
fm.Habitat: Near livestock, stables, dog runs — organic waste
fm.Diet: Blood — mammals including humans

desc: Floats through shaded woodland like a phantom. Inflated tarsi act as parachutes. Ethereal slow flight.

fm.Size: Body ⅓ in–½ in; legs very long with inflated tarsi
fm.Color: Black and white banded legs; inflated white tarsal segments
fm.Behavior: Drifts slowly through shade; inflated feet act as parachutes; ethereal
fm.Diet: Adults: do not feed. Larvae: aquatic

desc: One of the largest flies — wasp mimic with orange abdominal band. Impressive hovering. Larvae in rotting wood.

fm.Color: Black with orange second abdominal segment — wasp mimic
fm.Behavior: Powerful hoverer; visits flowers; Batesian wasp mimic
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: in rotting wood/soil (predatory)

desc: Black soldier fly. Larvae are champion composters — process organic waste rapidly. Commercially raised for waste management and animal feed.

fm.Color: Black; wasp-like; two translucent 'windows' on abdomen
fm.Behavior: Adults harmless; larvae consume organic waste; commercial composting
fm.Diet: Larvae: decomposing organic matter (champion composters)

desc: Dreaded invasive fruit pest. Periodic outbreaks trigger quarantine zones. NOT the tiny Drosophila vinegar fly.

fm.Color: Yellow-brown; patterned wings; large for a fruit fly
fm.Behavior: Lays eggs in ripening fruit; causes massive agricultural damage
fm.Diet: Larvae: ripening fruit — citrus, stone fruit, many others

desc: Invasive from Asia. Unlike other vinegar flies, attacks RIPE (not rotting) fruit. Males have dark wing spots. Major berry pest.

fm.Color: Tan; ♂ has dark spot on each wing (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Lays eggs in intact ripe fruit; devastating berry/cherry pest
fm.Habitat: Berry farms, orchards, gardens with fruit
fm.Diet: Larvae: ripe fruit — berries, cherries, grapes

desc: Giant, iridescent mosquito that does NOT bite humans. Larvae are predatory — eat other mosquito larvae. Beneficial.

fm.Size: ½ in–⅔ in (giant for a mosquito)
fm.Color: Metallic blue-green iridescence; curved proboscis (can't pierce skin)
fm.Behavior: Hovers near vegetation; does NOT bite; larvae eat mosquito larvae
fm.Habitat: Tree holes, containers — wherever standing water collects
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar only. Larvae: predatory on mosquito larvae (beneficial)

desc: Gray, sluggish fly that clusters in buildings fall/winter. Larvae parasitize earthworms. Not a filth fly.

fm.Color: Dark gray; golden hairs on thorax; sluggish
fm.Behavior: Mass clusters in buildings fall/winter; sluggish; not attracted to garbage
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize earthworms

desc: Attractive small fly with banded/patterned wings. Waves wings in display. On decaying vegetation.

fm.Color: Dark with patterned/banded wings; wing-waving behavior
fm.Behavior: Elaborate wing-waving display; on decaying plants
fm.Diet: Larvae: decaying plant material

desc: Small gray fly whose larvae are the 'root maggots' that damage Brassicaceae crops. Important agricultural pest.

fm.Color: Gray; similar to small house fly
fm.Behavior: Lays eggs at base of plants; larvae bore into roots
fm.Diet: Larvae: roots of Brassicaceae (major pest)

desc: Tiny, fuzzy, moth-like fly found in bathrooms. Breeds in drain biofilm. Also called drain fly. Harmless.

fm.Behavior: Sits on bathroom walls; breeds in drain biofilm; poor flier
fm.Diet: Larvae: biofilm in drains and sewage

desc: Tiny wingless fly that rides on honeybees. Not a true louse. Steals food from bee's mouth. Declining with bee declines.

fm.Color: Brown; round; wingless; resembles a mite
fm.Behavior: Rides on adult honeybees; steals food from bee's mouth (kleptoparasite)
fm.Diet: Kleptoparasite — steals food from honeybees

desc: Metallic green fly. Forensically important — one of first insects to colonize remains. Also causes myiasis in sheep.
Tiny biting midge causing seasonal misery in inland valleys. Breeds in moist soil. Worse at dawn/dusk.

fm.Size: Tiny biting midge causing seasonal misery in inland valleys

desc: Tiny fly that runs rapidly rather than flying. Breeds in decaying organic matter. Forensic indicator species.

fm.Size: Tiny fly that runs rapidly rather than flying

desc: Flat wingless fly parasitizing pigeons. Blood-feeder. Also called pigeon fly. Vectors avian malaria.
Tiny fuzzy fly breeding in sink and shower drains. Indicator of biofilm buildup. Harmless.

fm.Size: Tiny fuzzy fly breeding in sink and shower drains

desc: Small dark fly on windows. Larvae in fermenting organic matter. Sometimes called wood gnats.
Large bristly-faced robber fly ambushing other insects in flight. Powerful predator of bees and wasps.

fm.Prey: Bees, wasps, other flies — caught in flight

desc: Banded-wing biting fly. Females need blood for eggs. Iridescent green eyes. Near water.
Large bristly fly parasitizing caterpillars. Important natural biocontrol. Larvae develop inside host.
Gray-striped fly depositing live larvae on carrion. Forensically important. Three dark thoracic stripes.

fm.Color: Gray with three dark thoracic stripes
fm.Habitat: Carrion; also parasitic on other insects
fm.Behavior: Deposits live larvae not eggs (larviparous)

desc: Tiny metallic green fly on leaves. Males wave ornate leg flags in courtship. Predatory on small insects.

fm.Behavior: ♂ waves ornate leg flags in courtship display

desc: Small black fly found indoors on windows. Larvae predatory on carpet beetle larvae in carpets.

fm.Habitat: Small black fly found indoors on windows

desc: Predatory fly perching head-down on tree trunks. Pounces on passing insects. Pointed head.

fm.Size: Predatory fly perching head-down on tree trunks

desc: Small fly breeding in rotting kelp on beaches. Important decomposer. Huge swarms on wrack line.

fm.Habitat: Small fly breeding in rotting kelp on beaches

desc: Black fly swarming in spring. Males with huge eyes. Larvae feed on roots and decaying matter in soil.
Wasp-mimicking fly. Internal parasitoid of bumble bees — larva develops inside living bee.
World's most widespread fly. Pest and disease vector. Breeds in organic waste. Complex eyes with 4000 facets.

fm.Color: Gray with four dark thoracic stripes

desc: Tiny vinegar fly on ripening fruit. Most studied animal in genetics — Nobel Prizes won using this species.

fm.Role: Most important model organism in genetics

desc: One of the largest flies in NA. Ambushes large insects including dragonflies and bees. Powerful predator.
Tiny fly parasitizing fire ants. Lays egg in ant — larva decapitates host. Released as biocontrol of fire ants.
Large dark fly. Larvae develop as subcutaneous parasites in rodents and rabbits. Occasionally infests pets.

fm.Role: Parasitoid — larvae develop under host skin

desc: Small fly with banded wings. Larvae bore into cherries. Important stone fruit pest in West.
Small fly with banded wings. Larvae stain and damage walnut husks. Common wherever walnuts grow.
Fruit fly with distinctive wing pattern. Quarantine pest — not established in CA. Active detection program.

fm.Habitat: Apple orchards — quarantine pest in CA

desc: Devastating olive pest from Mediterranean. Arrived in CA 1998. Larvae tunnel through olive flesh.

fm.Habitat: Olive trees — established in CA since 1998

desc: Invasive ankle-biter established in LA County since 2011. Day-biting. Vectors dengue, Zika, chikungunya — first locally acquired dengue in LA history 2024.

fm.Size: ¼ in; black with white leg bands
fm.Habitat: Urban — breeds in any tiny water container
fm.Behavior: Aggressive daytime biter
fm.Disease: Vectors dengue, Zika, chikungunya, yellow fever

desc: Invasive black-and-white striped mosquito. Aggressive daytime biter. Established in parts of LA County. Vectors dengue and chikungunya.

fm.Size: ¼ in; black with single white dorsal stripe
fm.Habitat: Urban — any small container with standing water

desc: LA's most common native mosquito. Night-biting. Primary vector of West Nile virus in Southern California.

fm.Habitat: Storm drains, pools, any stagnant water
fm.Disease: Primary West Nile virus vector in SoCal

desc: Invasive Old World blow fly — most abundant blow fly in LA County. Metallic blue-green. Predatory larvae. Key forensic entomology species.
Invasive Old World blow fly. Metallic blue-green with dark anterior spiracle. Forensically important.
Tiny humpbacked biting fly breeding in flowing water. Target of LA River vector control programs. Females slash skin.

fm.Behavior: Females bite painfully; huge swarms near water

desc: Tiny fly whose larvae mine serpentine trails through leaves. Major greenhouse and agricultural pest.

fm.Damage: Larvae mine visible serpentine trails in leaves

desc: Small dark fly breeding in decomposing kelp on LA beaches. Essential coastal decomposer.

fm.Habitat: Sandy beaches — decomposing kelp wrack line

desc: The 'mosquito-like' insect that doesn't bite. Enormous swarms near water at dusk. Males have feathery antennae. Larvae ('bloodworms') are bright red.

fm.Behavior: Huge dusk swarms; does NOT bite

desc: The 'giant mosquito' that isn't — completely harmless. Introduced. Larvae ('leatherjackets') damage turf.

fm.Size: ½–⅔ in body; very long legs
fm.Behavior: Cannot bite or sting; clumsy flier

desc: The small fly that hovers in lazy triangular patterns under ceiling lights. Smaller and more slender than house fly.

fm.Size: ⅛–¼ in; smaller than house fly
fm.Behavior: Males hover in lazy triangles under lights

desc: Tiny dark fly swarming around houseplant soil. Larvae feed on fungi and roots in potting mix. #1 indoor plant pest fly.
Tiny fly that induces distinctive round bud galls on coyote bush. Galls far more conspicuous than the 1mm adults.

fm.Gall: Round bud galls on Baccharis — very conspicuous

desc: Large gray fly with three black thoracic stripes and checkered abdomen. Deposits live larvae (not eggs) on carrion and excrement. Common urban fly. Forensic indicator.

fm.Color: Gray with 3 black thoracic stripes; checkered abdomen
fm.Behavior: Deposits live larvae (larviparity)

desc: Slender tachinid with orange-red head and narrow waist. Parasitizes true bugs (Pentatomidae). A beneficial insect that helps control stink bugs.

fm.Color: Orange-red head; black body; narrow waist

desc: Fuzzy bee mimic with long fixed proboscis. Hovers at flowers. Larvae are parasitoids of solitary bee nests. Flicks eggs into bee nest entrances while hovering.

fm.Behavior: Flicks eggs into solitary bee nests while hovering

desc: Large flat wasp-mimic fly with yellow abdominal markings. Larvae aquatic in stagnant water. Adults visit flowers. Important pollinator.

fm.Color: Black with yellow abdominal spots; flat body
fm.Habitat: Near water — larvae aquatic; adults on flowers

desc: Tiny brilliant metallic green fly on leaf surfaces. Males perform elaborate courtship displays waving modified tarsi. Predatory on tiny insects. Very common in gardens.

fm.Habitat: Leaf surfaces in gardens — tiny predator
fm.Behavior: ♂ elaborate courtship with modified feet

desc: Wasp-mimic fly with enlarged head. Parasitoid — female intercepts bumblebees in flight, inserts egg into abdomen. Larva develops inside living bee. Remarkable biology.

fm.Color: Black and yellow wasp mimic; enlarged head
fm.Behavior: Parasitizes living bumblebees — injects egg in flight

desc: Similar to house fly but eyes closer together (♀) or touching (♂). Feeds on eye and nose secretions of livestock. Enters houses in fall seeking overwintering sites.

fm.Color: Gray; eyes closer together than house fly
fm.Habitat: Near livestock — feeds on facial secretions
fm.Behavior: Invades homes in fall for winter shelter

desc: Small fly with striking dark-patterned wings. Waves wings in semaphore-like courtship display on leaves. Breeds in decaying vegetation. Common on compost.

fm.Behavior: Waves wings in semaphore display on leaf surfaces

desc: Small dark fly of alkaline lakes and saline pools. Larvae feed on algae in mineral-rich water. Important food source for shorebirds at Mono Lake (and similar LA County playas).
Tiny fly attracted to eyes and wounds. Persistent and annoying. Can transmit pinkeye. The classic 'annoying SoCal gnat.'
Extraordinary fly that mimics ichneumon wasps — walks on front 4 legs, waves black-tipped forelegs like antennae. Remarkable behavioral mimicry.

fm.Behavior: Waves forelegs like wasp antennae — behavioral mimic

desc: Elongated yellow-and-black soldier fly. Larvae in compost. Adults visit flowers. Not related to BSF but same family.
Large bristly fly. Parasitizes cutworm and armyworm caterpillars. Important agricultural biocontrol agent.
Golden-haired fly on vegetation. Predatory. Often hangs from leaves head-down waiting to pounce on passing insects.
Tiny shiny black fly that waves its wings constantly while walking. Common on dung and compost. Distinctive wing-waving courtship display.

fm.Behavior: Constantly waves wings while walking

desc: Tiny dark fly breeding in fermenting organic matter, drains, and compost. Very common indoors. Often confused with fungus gnats.
Transparent aquatic larvae ('glass worms') in ponds — nearly invisible predators. Adults at lights near water. Related to mosquitoes but don't bite.
Large robber fly — spectacular bumblebee mimic with dense golden-brown pile. Aggressive aerial predator catching insects in flight. Very common in SoCal gardens and scrubland.

fm.Behavior: Catches prey in flight; perches on exposed twigs

desc: Brilliant metallic green long-legged fly. Males perform elaborate wing-waving courtship on sunlit leaves. One of the most commonly photographed tiny flies. Predatory on mites and gnats.

fm.Behavior: ♂ wing-waving courtship on sunlit leaves

desc: Four-speckled hover fly — small syrphid with four dark wing spots. Very common at flowers. Larvae prey on aphids. One of the most abundant hover flies in western gardens.
Boatman fly — distinctive stilt-legged fly with patterned wings that waves its front legs like antennae. Mimics parasitoid wasps. Found on decaying vegetation.

fm.Behavior: Waves front legs like wasp antennae

desc: White-bowed smoothwing hover fly — medium syrphid with distinctive white comma marks on dark abdomen. Strong flier — can hover motionlessly. Larvae devour aphids.

fm.Color: Dark with white comma marks on abdomen

desc: Large tiger crane fly — yellow-and-black patterned. Despite fearsome appearance, completely harmless. Larvae in soil. Very common at lights on warm evenings.
Sunflower seed maggot — small picture-winged fly. Larvae develop inside sunflower seed heads. Banded wings waved in courtship display. Common wherever sunflowers grow.
Large hover fly — excellent wasp mimic. Visits flowers aggressively. Larvae develop in rotting cactus tissue. Common at desert-urban interface. Harmless despite wasp appearance.

fm.Color: Dark with yellow bands — wasp mimic

desc: Avid hover fly — large hover fly and excellent wasp mimic. Visits flowers. Larvae develop in rotting cactus tissue. Common at desert-urban interface.

fm.Color: Dark with yellow bands — wasp mimic

desc: Haag's hover fly — large showy hover fly. Bumblebee mimic. Larvae in rotting agave and cactus. Common in gardens with succulents. Harmless.

fm.Color: Dense black and golden pile — bumblebee mimic

desc: Cactus fly — medium tachinid parasitoid. Found in desert and chaparral habitats. Important natural enemy of caterpillar pests.
Small-tailed hover fly — medium syrphid visiting flowers. Important pollinator and aphid predator. Common in gardens and agricultural areas.
Sage gall midge — tiny fly inducing conspicuous round galls on white sage (Salvia apiana) stems. Galls are diagnostic — round swellings on sage stems. Very common in sage scrub.

fm.Color: Tiny; round stem galls on white sage

desc: March fly — small chunky black fly swarming in spring. Males with enormous eyes. Slow hovering swarms often mistaken for gnats. Larvae decompose soil organic matter.
Mexican hover fly — large hover fly with bee-like coloring. Common flower visitor. Larvae develop in stagnant water. Important pollinator.
Alhambra hover fly — large hover fly named after Alhambra, California. Bee mimic. Visits flowers. Larvae in decaying organic matter in water.
Narrow-winged long-legged fly — small metallic green dolichopodid on stream margins and moist surfaces. Predatory on tiny insects. Very common near water.
Root maggot fly — small gray muscoid fly. Larvae mine roots and stems of various plants. Common in gardens. Often found at windows.

fm.Color: Gray; similar to small house fly

desc: European crane fly — large crane fly introduced from Europe. Larvae ('leatherjackets') damage turf roots. Adults at lights. Harmless — does not bite. Often feared unnecessarily.

fm.Color: Gray-brown; huge wings relative to body

desc: Woolly sage gall midge — tiny fly inducing conspicuous woolly white galls on sage (Artemisia). Galls are fuzzy white balls on sage stems. Very common in sage scrub.

fm.Color: Tiny; woolly white galls on sage

desc: Yellow lauxaniid fly — small bright yellow fly on vegetation. Larvae in decaying plant matter. Very common in gardens. Often on compost.
Tiger bee fly — large bee fly with spotted wings. Parasitoid of carpenter bee larvae. Hovers near carpenter bee nests. One of the most striking bee flies.

fm.Color: Dark with bold wing spots; furry body

desc: Honeysuckle fly — small tephritid fruit fly specializing on honeysuckle berries. Larvae develop inside fruit. Common wherever ornamental honeysuckle grows.
Arethusa bee fly — medium bee fly with ornate wing patterns. Larvae parasitize moth pupae. Hovers near ground in sandy habitats. Beautiful wing markings.
Window-winged stiletto fly — medium therevid with distinctive wing markings. Larvae predatory in soil. Adults found on sandy ground and trails.
Conopid fly — thick-headed fly that parasitizes bees. Female intercepts bees in flight and inserts egg. Larva develops inside living host.
Slow hover fly — large hover fly visiting flowers. Cactus-associated — larvae develop in rotting cactus tissue. Bee mimic.

fm.Color: Dark with pale bands — bee mimic

desc: Golden-haired gall midge — tiny fly inducing bud galls on various plants. Galls are swollen distorted buds. Common on chaparral shrubs.
Violet hover fly — large metallic violet-blue hover fly. Spectacular coloring. Larvae in rotting cactus. Visits flowers. Desert-urban interface.
Hairy-footed hover fly — small dark syrphid. Males with modified front tarsi for grasping during mating. Larvae eat aphids. Common in gardens.

fm.Color: Dark with pale side marks; ♂ modified feet

desc: Golden robber fly — medium-sized robber fly with golden thoracic pile. Perches on rocks and bare ground, darting out to catch passing insects in flight.
Bee fly with dark wing patches. Common on sandy soil where larvae parasitize moth pupae. Hovers near ground. Dark wing pattern distinguishes from Bombylius.
Immigrant fruit fly — medium-large Drosophila introduced worldwide. Darker than D. melanogaster. Found on rotting fruit year-round. Important genetics research organism.

fm.Color: Brown; darker than common fruit fly

desc: Barber's midge — non-biting chironomid midge. Larvae aquatic — important fish food. Adults form swarms near water. Part of the dominant stream insect community.
Woolly bud gall midge — tiny fly inducing conspicuous woolly galls on sagebrush (Artemisia) buds. Galls resemble tiny cotton balls. Very common in sage scrub.

fm.Color: Tiny; woolly cotton-ball galls on sagebrush buds

desc: Peg-legged compost fly — small hover fly with thickened hind femora. Breeds in compost and decaying vegetation. Very common in gardens. Males hover aggressively.

fm.Color: Dark with yellow ventral markings; thick hind legs

desc: Deceptive hover fly — large hover fly mimicking a wasp. Visits flowers. Larvae develop in rotting cactus and agave. Common at desert-urban interface.
Black-footed drone fly — medium hover fly with hairy legs and bee-like appearance. Rat-tailed maggot larvae in stagnant water. Common at flowers.
March fly — small chunky black fly swarming in spring. Slow hovering swarms. Larvae feed on decomposing plant matter in soil. Important soil decomposer.
Sponge gall midge — tiny fly inducing spongy round galls on sagebrush stems. Galls are light, pithy, and persist on dead stems. Very common in sage scrub.

fm.Color: Tiny; spongy round galls on sagebrush

desc: Mallow leaf miner — tiny agromyzid fly whose larvae create serpentine mines in mallow and hollyhock leaves. White winding trails on leaves are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Dark; serpentine leaf mines on mallows

desc: Spotted-wing hover fly — large hover fly with dark wing spots. Cactus-associated larvae. Visits flowers. Desert and foothill habitats.

fm.Color: Dark with wing spots; on flowers

desc: Glabrous frit fly — tiny yellow chloropid fly. Can form massive aggregations in buildings during fall. Thousands may cluster in attics and window frames.

fm.Behavior: Mass aggregations in buildings — fall

desc: Amazon midge — non-biting chironomid midge. Larvae in organically rich water. Adults at lights near water. Important water quality indicator species.
Cold weather mosquito — large mosquito active in cooler months when other species are dormant. Does not vector disease. Breeds in tree holes and artificial containers.
Spiny tachina fly — large bristly tachinid parasitizing caterpillars. Covered in strong spines/bristles. Important natural enemy of cutworms and armyworms.
Willow rosette gall midge — tiny fly inducing cabbage-like rosette galls at willow branch tips. Galls resemble small green roses. Very common on willows.

fm.Color: Tiny; cabbage-rosette galls on willow tips

desc: Striped mosquito — introduced from Australia. Aggressive daytime biter. White-striped legs. Breeds in small containers. Expanding in SoCal since ~2020.
Ceanothus bud gall midge — tiny fly inducing enlarged bud galls on ceanothus (California lilac). Galls are swollen distorted buds. Very common in chaparral.

fm.Color: Tiny; swollen bud galls on ceanothus

desc: Nugator bee fly — medium bee fly with dark wing patches. Larvae parasitize moth pupae in soil. Hovers near sandy ground in desert and scrub habitats.

fm.Color: Gray; dark wing patches; hovers near ground

desc: Hornworm tachina fly — large tachinid parasitoid of tomato hornworms (Manduca). Important agricultural biocontrol agent. Bristly with orange abdominal tip.
Black bee fly — dark bee fly common in sandy habitats. Larvae parasitize solitary bee and wasp nests. Hovers near ground scouting host burrows.
Western wasp fly — elongated hover fly that strongly mimics paper wasps. Narrow waist and wasp coloring. Harmless. Visits flowers.
Common lagoon fly — medium hover fly with spotted eyes. Found near brackish water and coastal wetlands. Larvae in stagnant saline water. Bee-like appearance.

fm.Color: Bee-mimic; spotted compound eyes; near salt water

desc: Serpentine bee fly — bee fly with sinuous dark wing markings. Hovers near ground. Larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees and wasps. Desert and foothill habitats.
Wonderful bee fly — medium bee fly visiting flowers in desert and foothill scrub. Fuzzy. Long proboscis for nectar feeding. Larvae parasitize other insects.
Western American deer ked — flat wingless parasitic fly on deer. Breaks off wings after finding host. Blood-feeder. Can accidentally land on hikers. Does not establish on humans.

fm.Behavior: Breaks off wings after landing on deer host

desc: Single-spotted dimpleback — hover fly with distinctive single wing spot and dimpled thorax. Visits flowers. Uncommon but distinctive.

fm.Color: Dark with single wing spot; dimpled thorax

desc: Toyon fruit gall midge — tiny fly inducing galls in toyon (Heteromeles) fruit. Fruits become swollen and deformed. Very common in chaparral wherever toyon grows.
Western march fly — small dark bibionid swarming in spring. Sluggish hovering flight. Larvae decompose organic matter in soil. Important soil health indicator.
African fig fly — invasive fruit fly from Africa spreading rapidly through SoCal since ~2005. Distinctive white head stripes. Attacks ripening figs. Can damage stone fruit.
Neighboring picture-wing fly — small tephritid on marigold and other Asteraceae flower heads. Wings with ornate banding pattern waved in courtship displays.
Bubble-gall fruit fly — small tephritid inducing conspicuous bubble-like galls on sagebrush stems. Host-specific to Artemisia. Patterned wings.
Texas thick-headed fly — conopid parasitoid of bumblebees and other large bees. Female intercepts bees in flight, injects egg. Larva develops inside living host.
Common sickleleg — medium hover fly with curved hind femora. Larvae in decaying organic matter. Common at flowers in chaparral.

fm.Color: Dark with pale markings; curved hind legs

desc: Blue blowfly — large metallic blue fly. One of the first insects colonizing remains — key forensic species. Adults enter houses. Breeds in carrion and pet waste.
Mixed bee fly — medium bee fly with dark wing patches. Hovers at flowers. Larvae parasitize moth pupae in soil. Common in sandy foothill habitats.

fm.Color: Gray-brown; dark wing patches; hovers at flowers

desc: Crowned soldier fly — medium flat stratiomyid with pale markings. Adults on flowers. Larvae aquatic in shallow water and wet soil.

fm.Color: Dark with pale crown-like head markings

desc: Sand hammertail — medium robber fly with white facial beard ('albibarbis'). Perches on sand and bare ground, darting out to catch passing insects. Sandy washes.
Truqui's soldier fly — flat dark stratiomyid. Adults visit flowers. Larvae in moist soil and decaying vegetation. Part of the diverse soldier fly family.

fm.Color: Dark; flat body — typical soldier fly

desc: Saltbush tumor gall midge — tiny cecidomyiid inducing swollen tumor-like stem galls on saltbush (Atriplex). Very common in desert scrub and coastal sage.
Dusty snipe fly — medium fly hanging head-down on tree trunks and fence posts waiting to pounce on passing prey. Long legs. Woodland and riparian areas.

fm.Behavior: Hangs head-down on trunks; pounces on prey

desc: Narrow-banded picture-winged fly — small fly with ornately patterned wings. Waves wings in courtship displays on leaf surfaces. In decaying vegetation.
Striate-fronted lance fly — small shiny black fly. Larvae in decaying vegetation and damaged fruit. Attracted to fermenting matter.
Fourwing saltbush woolly gall midge — tiny fly inducing fluffy woolly galls on fourwing saltbush (Atriplex canescens). Galls conspicuous white tufts on stems.
Western band-winged hover fly — hover fly with distinctive dark wing band. Visits flowers in woodland and riparian areas.
Plumed fungus gnat — small delicate fly of woodland habitats. Larvae feed on fungi in rotting wood. Adults attracted to lights near forest.
Herringbone leafminer — tiny agromyzid creating distinctive herringbone-pattern mines in lantana leaves. Introduced for biocontrol of invasive lantana.

fm.Color: Tiny; herringbone leaf mines on lantana

desc: Longhorn cactus fly — spectacular neriid fly with very long antennae. Breeds in rotting cactus. Male-male combat with jousting. Stilt-like legs.

fm.Color: Brown; very long antennae; stilt legs
fm.Behavior: Males joust for mating rights

desc: Artichoke fly — small tephritid whose larvae develop inside artichoke flower heads, consuming developing seeds. Pest of commercial artichoke production. Ornate wing pattern.
Yellow-legged root maggot fly — small gray fly with pale legs. Larvae in soil feeding on roots and decaying plant matter. Common in gardens.
Two-bristled picture-wing fly — small tephritid with ornate banded wing pattern. Larvae in Asteraceae flower heads. Wings waved in courtship display.
American stiletto robber fly — very slender elongated robber fly. Aerial predator catching small insects in flight. Extremely thin body. Perches on twigs.
Cultivated fruit fly — small tephritid with banded wings. Larvae in Asteraceae seed heads. Wings displayed in elaborate courtship dances.
Lupine gall midge — tiny fly inducing leaf-fold or leaf-roll galls on native lupines. Host-specific. Common where lupines grow in chaparral.
Common tiger fly — small predatory muscid. Remarkable — catches other flies in midair from perch on vegetation. Important natural enemy of fungus gnats in greenhouses.

fm.Behavior: Predatory — catches flies in midair from perch

desc: Black-horned picture-wing fly — small tephritid with dark antennae and patterned wings. In composite flower heads. Part of the diverse tephritid community.
Busck's fruit fly — medium Drosophila associated with decaying fruit and vegetables. More cold-tolerant than D. melanogaster. Common in compost and garbage areas.
Lantana leaf blotch miner — tiny agromyzid creating characteristic blotch mines on lantana leaves. Introduced for biocontrol of invasive lantana in some regions.
Polished beach fly — tiny pale fly of sandy habitats and coastal dunes. Larvae in sand among decaying seaweed. Part of a rarely noticed fly family.
Little picture-wing fly — tiny tephritid with delicately patterned wings. On Asteraceae. Wings waved in display. Part of the species-rich fruit fly family.
Delila bee fly — large black bee fly with dark smoky wings. Parasitoid of carpenter bee larvae. Hovers near carpenter bee nesting wood scouting for hosts.

fm.Color: Black; smoky dark wings; near carpenter bee nests

desc: Cottony gall midge — tiny cecidomyiid inducing distinctive cotton-like woolly galls on sagebrush. Common in sage scrub habitats throughout SoCal.
Golden copperback — stunning metallic golden-copper hover fly. One of the most beautiful syrphids. Larvae in tree sap runs. Uncommon.
Silvestri's gall midge — tiny cecidomyiid inducing leaf-fold galls on native plants. Named for Italian entomologist Filippo Silvestri. Host-specific.
Bare-bristled fly — gray muscoid fly found in tropical and subtropical areas. Important forensic species — larvae develop in remains. Indoor-associated.
Sturtevant's shore fly — tiny ephydrid of pond margins and wet areas. Larvae in aquatic algal mats. Very common near any standing water.
Tufted fungus gnat — small fungus gnat of woodland habitats. Larvae develop in mushrooms and bracket fungi. Adults attracted to lights near wooded areas.
Western lauxaniid — small yellow-tan fly on shaded vegetation. Larvae in decaying leaves. Part of the diverse but overlooked Lauxaniidae family.
Western encephalitis mosquito — the most important mosquito disease vector in western North America. Vectors West Nile virus, western equine encephalitis. Breeds in irrigated agriculture.

fm.Warning: Primary West Nile virus vector in western US

desc: Femoral picture-wing fly — small tephritid. Larvae develop inside composite flower heads consuming seeds. Patterned wings used in courtship displays.
Doris bee fly — medium bee fly with elaborate wing pattern. Hovers near ground. Larvae parasitize solitary bee or wasp nests. Desert and foothill habitats.

fm.Color: Brown; ornate wing pattern; hovers near ground

desc: Vain bee fly — medium dark bee fly. Hovers near ground scouting host burrows. Larvae parasitize ground-nesting bee and wasp nests. Sandy habitats.

fm.Color: Dark; hovers near ground in sandy areas

desc: Willow potato gall midge — tiny fly inducing potato-shaped galls on willow stems. Each gall houses a single larva. Common on various willows.
Common loopwing aphideater — small hover fly with distinctive wing venation forming a loop. Larvae eat aphids. Common in mountain meadows.

fm.Color: Black with yellow markings; looped wing vein

desc: Interrupted hornet fly — large hover fly mimicking bald-faced hornets. Black and white pattern. Waves antennae like a wasp. Harmless. Visits flowers.
Dark bee fly — dark-winged bee fly of foothill habitats. Larval hyperparasitoid (parasitizes other parasitoids). Hovers at flowers and near ground.
Pleasant fly — small drosophilid breeding in decaying fruit and tree sap flows. Often found in association with fermenting fallen fruit.
Silver-banded hover fly — hover fly with distinctive silver-white abdominal bands. Visits flowers. Larvae eat aphids. Mountain and foothill habitats.
Fluke's mucksucker — small dark hover fly of wetland habitats. Larvae in muddy organic sediment. Adults visit flowers near water.
Black-handed robber fly — small dark asilid. Perches on rocks and bare ground. Darts out to catch small flying insects. Desert and scrub habitats.

fm.Color: Dark; perches on rocks; aerial predator

desc: Marked beach fly — tiny fly of sandy coastal and inland habitats. Related to T. polita. Larvae in sandy soil with decaying organic matter.
Rabbitbrush gall midge — tiny cecidomyiid inducing conspicuous galls on rabbitbrush (Chrysothamnus/Ericameria) stems. Galls are swollen stem growths. Very common in scrub.
Thistle leaf miner — small anthomyiid whose larvae mine inside thistle leaves creating blotch mines. Potential biocontrol agent for invasive thistles.
Temperate leaf miner — tiny agromyzid whose larvae create serpentine mines in leaves of various plants. White winding trails on leaves diagnostic.
Eye-bearing root maggot fly — small gray anthomyiid. Larvae develop in decaying organic matter and plant roots. At flowers. Common in gardens.
Early Tachinid Fly. Tachinid fly. Important parasitoid of caterpillars. Bristly body.
Maize Calligrapher. Hover fly. Bee mimic. Larvae eat aphids. Important pollinator.
Scaly bee fly — dark bee fly with scaled body. Larval hyperparasitoid — parasitizes other parasitoids inside their hosts. Hovers at ground level scouting for hosts.
Painted-footed robber fly — medium asilid with distinctively patterned tarsi. Aerial predator catching small insects in flight. Desert washes and sandy habitats.
Hairy soldier fly — small dark stratiomyid with dense body hair. Near water. Larvae aquatic. Part of the diverse soldier fly family.
Hydei fruit fly — large Drosophila (for the genus). Commonly used in genetics labs and as pet food (flightless culture). On rotting fruit. Larger than D. melanogaster.

fm.Color: Brown; larger than common fruit fly

desc: Hairy-legged crane fly — small crane fly of moist habitats. Long legs with dense hair. Larvae in decaying organic matter in wet soil.

fm.Size: ¼ in body; long hairy legs

desc: Jacaranda leaf miner — tiny agromyzid fly whose larvae mine inside jacaranda leaves. Serpentine mines visible as white trails. Common wherever jacarandas grow in LA.

fm.Color: Tiny; serpentine mines on jacaranda leaves

desc: White-headed Bee Fly. Bee fly. Hovers at flowers. Larvae parasitize ground-nesting insects.
Northern bee fly — small bombyliid visiting flowers. Larvae parasitize ground-nesting bee and wasp nests. Hovers near burrow entrances on sandy ground.
Daylily leafminer — tiny agromyzid whose larvae mine serpentine trails inside daylily leaves. Invasive pest first detected in CA ~2006. Can damage ornamental daylilies.
Western tachinid — medium bristly parasitoid fly. Larvae develop inside host caterpillars, killing them. Important natural enemy of agricultural pest caterpillars.
Arnaud's fungus gnat — small dark fungus gnat of woodland habitats. Larvae feed on fungi. Adults at lights. Part of the diverse but overlooked fungus gnat fauna.

fm.Color: Dark; in woodland; larvae on fungi

desc: Juniper Urn Gall Midge. Gall midge. Tiny. Induces plant galls. Many species specific to host plants.
Mormon Tea Stem Gall Midge. Gall midge. Tiny. Induces plant galls. Many species specific to host plants.
Tip-bearing cactus fly — large hover fly whose rat-tailed larvae develop in decaying cactus tissue. Adults are effective bee mimics visiting flowers.
Equal picture-wing fly — small tephritid on Asteraceae. Larvae develop in composite flower heads. Wings with banded pattern used in courtship display.
Precise crane fly — medium crane fly with very long delicate legs. Harmless despite alarming appearance. Larvae ('leatherjackets') in soil. Adults near moist habitats.
Fiddleneck Midge Gall. Gall midge. Tiny. Induces plant galls. Many species specific to host plants.
Frick's leafminer — tiny agromyzid creating serpentine mines in various plant leaves. Part of the economically important Liriomyza leafminer genus.
California marine midge — remarkable chironomid whose larvae are fully marine — living in rocky intertidal zone. One of the few truly marine insects. Adults tiny, short-lived.

fm.Note: One of the only marine insects

desc: Y-marked picture-wing fly — small tephritid with Y-shaped wing marking. On Asteraceae. Larvae consume developing seeds in flower heads.
Knowlton's soldier fly — small dark stratiomyid. Larvae in decaying organic matter. Adults on flowers. Part of the diverse soldier fly family.
Many-spotted crane fly — delicate crane fly with speckled wings. Moist habitats. Larvae in soil. Long delicate legs.

fm.Size: ½ in body; very long legs

desc: Striped fungus gnat — small dark fungus gnat with striped pattern. Larvae feed on fungi in woodland leaf litter. At lights near forested areas.
Saltbush Woolly Stem Gall Midge. Gall midge. Tiny. Induces plant galls. Many species specific to host plants.
Costal bee fly — medium bombyliid with dark costal wing margin. Parasitoid of ground-nesting insects. On flowers in sandy foothill habitats.
Rabbit bot fly — large dark fly whose larvae are internal parasites of rabbits. Huge larvae grow under host's skin. Adults rarely seen — don't feed. Dramatic parasitoid biology.

fm.Behavior: Larvae parasitize rabbits subcutaneously

desc: Marked-wing conopid — thick-headed fly parasitizing bees and wasps. Female intercepts host in flight. Larva develops inside living host. Wasp-like appearance.
Destitute bee fly — dark-winged bombyliid of foothill habitats. Larval hyperparasitoid. At flowers. Part of the species-rich bee fly family.
Pacific muscid — small bristly fly. Predatory — adults catch small insects. Common in woodland and gardens.
Black fig fly — small dark lonchaeoid whose larvae infest figs. Can cause premature fruit drop. Pest of backyard fig trees. Females oviposit through ostiole.
Ruddy robber fly — medium reddish-brown asilid. Perches on rocks and bare ground. Darts out to intercept flying insects. Chaparral and scrub habitats.
Black-thighed anthomyiid — small gray fly with dark femora. Larvae in decaying organic matter. At flowers.
Chamise gall midge — tiny fly inducing flower galls on chamise (Adenostoma). Very host-specific. Common wherever chamise dominates the chaparral.
Willow Pinecone Gall Midge. Gall midge. Tiny. Induces plant galls. Many species specific to host plants.
Translucent hover fly — medium syrphid with partly translucent wings. Larvae feed on aphids in root areas. Adults visit flowers. Important pollinator.
Bulbous window fly — small dark scenopinid. Larvae predatory in household carpets — eat carpet beetle and clothes moth larvae. Beneficial indoors.
Loew's bee fly — small bombyliid named for prominent dipterist Hermann Loew. Larvae parasitize soil-nesting insects. Adults hover at flowers.
Channel bee fly — medium bombyliid of sandy washes and stream channels. Larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees and wasps. Hovers near ground.
Emerald small-headed fly — striking metallic green acrocerid. Larvae are internal parasites of spiders. Adults visit flowers. Bizarre biology — parasitoid of spiders.

fm.Behavior: Larvae parasitize spiders internally

desc: Dark fruit fly — medium-large Drosophila with spotted wings. On rotting fruit and fermenting vegetation. Common in kitchens and compost bins.
Star-bearing picture-wing fly — small tephritid with star-shaped wing markings. On composite flowers. Larvae develop in flower heads. Ornate wing display.
Wheeler's picture-wing fly — small tephritid on Asteraceae. Named for entomologist Wheeler. Patterned wings displayed in courtship. Larvae in flower heads.
Yellow lauxaniid — small bright yellow fly. On vegetation in gardens. Larvae in decaying plant material.
Bermudagrass Stem Maggot. Muscid fly. Some species bite. Common around habitation.
Creosote Cone Gall Midge. Gall midge. Tiny. Induces plant galls. Many species specific to host plants.
Handsome midge — non-biting chironomid midge. Larvae aquatic. Adults at lights near water. Part of the dominant freshwater insect community.
Yellowish trixoscelid — tiny pale fly on sandy habitats. Part of the poorly known trixoscelid fly family.
Remarkable flat-footed fly — small platypezid fly. Males have expanded/flattened hind tarsi used in courtship display. Larvae in fungi.

fm.Color: Dark; ♂ flattened hind feet for display

desc: Dark-winged root maggot fly — small gray fly with darkened wings. Larvae in decaying vegetation and roots. Part of the diverse root maggot fly family.
Distinct crane fly — small delicate crane fly of moist habitats. Larvae in wet soil and decaying vegetation. Long fragile legs.
Bracket fungus fly — small flat-footed fly whose larvae develop inside bracket fungi on trees. Host-specific to wood-decay fungi.
Golden-horned Chiselmouth. Robber fly. Aerial predator. Catches insects in flight.
Mountain Mahogany gall midge. Gall midge. Tiny. Induces plant galls. Many species specific to host plants.
Stigmatic picture-wing fly — small tephritid with dark wing stigma mark. On composite flowers. Part of the diverse fruit fly community on chaparral Asteraceae.
Santa Maria feverfew gall midge — tiny fly inducing flower galls on feverfew (Parthenium). Host-specific. Part of the diverse cecidomyiid community.
Broad-winged leafminer — tiny agromyzid creating blotch mines in Asteraceae leaves. Named for broad wings. On native composites in chaparral.
Encelia leafminer — tiny agromyzid making blotch mines specifically on encelia (brittlebush) leaves. Host-specific to this common chaparral shrub.
Yerba santa leafminer — tiny agromyzid mining in Eriodictyon (yerba santa) leaves. Host-specific to this native chaparral shrub. Serpentine mine pattern.
Fleabane gall midge — tiny fly inducing rosette galls on fleabane (Erigeron). Creates dense clumps of shortened leaves. Common on roadsides.
Tuberculate fruit fly — medium drosophilid with distinctive head tubercles. African origin. Recently established in SoCal. On rotting fruit.
Tiny midge — minute chironomid. Larvae aquatic in ponds and slow streams. Adults at lights. Non-biting.
Unbridled bee fly — dark-winged bombyliid. Larvae parasitize moth pupae in soil. Adults at flowers and on bare ground in sandy habitats.
Cottony cushion scale parasitoid — tiny fly that was the world's first successful classical biocontrol agent (1888). Parasitizes cottony cushion scale. Historic importance.
Slender hunter fly — small predatory muscid that catches whiteflies and fungus gnats in flight. Important biocontrol agent in greenhouses. Both adults and larvae predatory.
Rosette Bud Gall Midge. Gall midge. Tiny. Induces plant galls. Many species specific to host plants.
Barber's heleomyzid — small fly associated with fungi and decaying vegetation. In woodland habitats. Part of the fungus-associated fly community.
Sourbush Seed Fly. Fruit fly with patterned wings. Larvae in fruit or flower heads.
Dark-winged stiletto fly — medium therevid. Larvae predatory in soil. Adults on sandy ground. Part of the diverse stiletto fly fauna.
Edwards' bee fly — medium bee fly visiting flowers in desert scrub. Named for Henry Edwards. Larvae likely parasitize solitary bees.
Hairy soldier fly — small dark hairy stratiomyid. Near water. Larvae aquatic. Part of the diverse soldier fly family.
California fuchsia gall midge. Gall midge. Tiny. Induces plant galls. Many species specific to host plants.
Acton mydas fly — large spectacular mydas fly. One of the largest flies in North America. Desert species. Larvae in soil. Adults visit flowers. Bee mimic.

fm.Color: Dark with pale bands; bee mimic; desert

desc: Slow fungus gnat — tiny dark-winged fungus gnat. Larvae in damp soil feeding on fungi and roots. Can damage potted plants. Common in greenhouses.

fm.Color: Dark; tiny; in damp soil/potting mix

desc: Western sap fly — small fly found at tree sap flows. Breeds in fermenting tree sap. Red eyes distinctive. Found at wounded tree bark.

fm.Color: Dark; red eyes; at tree sap flows

desc: Brown-haltered Bee-mimic Fly. Hover fly. Bee mimic. Larvae eat aphids. Important pollinator.
Western treehole mosquito — breeds in water-filled tree holes, stumps, and artificial containers. Can vector dog heartworm. Bites in woodland habitats during day.
Sage gall midge — tiny fly inducing stem galls on native sage (Salvia). Very common — look for swollen stem growths on white sage and black sage.
Livingston's lauxaniid — small fly on vegetation. Larvae in decaying plant matter. Part of diverse but poorly known lauxaniid fauna.
Secondary screwworm — metallic green blow fly that lays eggs in wounds on animals. Unlike primary screwworm, only attacks existing wounds. Forensically important.
Horse-eating black fly — small dark biting fly of streams. Aggressive biters of livestock. Larvae in fast-flowing streams attached to rocks.
Tawny-haired bee fly — medium bee fly with tawny/golden hair. At flowers. Larvae parasitize moth pupae.
Bomber fly — medium robust bee fly visiting flowers aggressively. Larvae parasitize alkali bee nests. Important pollinator despite parasitoid habits.
California bee fly — medium bee fly with ornate wing pattern. California native. At flowers and hovering near ground.
Hairy stiletto fly — medium therevid with dense body hair. Larvae predatory in soil. On sandy habitats.
Desert broom gallfly — small tephritid inducing bud galls on desert broom (Baccharis sarothroides). Swollen buds diagnostic. Desert habitat.

fm.Color: Pale with patterned wings; galls on desert broom

desc: Baccharis tephritid — small picture-winged fly on coyote brush (Baccharis). Larvae in flower heads.
Curled fungus gnat — small dark mycetophilid whose larvae develop inside mushrooms and other fungi. At lights near wooded areas. Part of the fungus-gnat community.
Baccharis stem gall midge — tiny fly inducing stem galls on coyote brush (Baccharis). Swollen stem sections.

fm.Color: Tiny; stem galls on coyote brush

desc: Jones' bee fly — medium bee fly visiting flowers. Named for entomologist R.A. Jones. Desert and scrub habitats.
Black blow fly — metallic dark blue-green blow fly. One of the first insects to colonize carrion. Critical forensic indicator species for time-of-death estimation.
Red frit fly — tiny reddish chloropid. Can form massive aggregations in buildings. Related to T. glabra. In attics and window frames.

fm.Color: Reddish; tiny; mass aggregations in buildings

desc: Ochre parasitoid fly — remarkable tachinid that locates cricket hosts by sound using specialized ear-like hearing organs. Larvae consume cricket internally. Subject of biomimetic research.

fm.Note: Locates crickets by sound — ears studied for hearing aid technology

desc: Hairy soldier fly — medium stratiomyid covered in fine hairs. Near water. Larvae aquatic.
Vestige-winged crane fly — small crane fly with reduced wing markings. In moist habitats. Larvae in soil.
Blessed asteia — tiny pale fly. Part of the poorly known Asteiidae family. On vegetation.
Monkeyflower leaf miner — tiny agromyzid mining in monkeyflower (Mimulus/Diplacus) leaves. Host-specific native miner.
Victoria's hover fly — large hover fly. Cactus-associated larvae. On flowers. Named for location.
Hairy-eyed soldier fly — medium stratiomyid with hairy eyes. Near water. Larvae aquatic.
Petroleum fly — remarkable midge whose larvae develop in CRUDE OIL seeps. One of the few organisms adapted to petroleum. Found at La Brea and other SoCal oil seeps.

fm.Note: Larvae live in crude oil — extraordinary

desc: California muscid — small gray fly. Predatory or on decaying material. Part of the diverse muscid fauna.
Western dance fly — small empidid. Dance flies are named for males' courtship swarms where they present prey gifts to females.
Flexible long-legged fly — small metallic dolichopodid. Predatory on tiny insects. Very fast.
Five-marked midge — non-biting chironomid. Larvae aquatic. Adults at lights near water.
Variable-winged fungus gnat — small dark mycetophilid. Larvae feed on fungi in leaf litter. At lights.
Cross-marked dung fly — tiny sphaerocerid on dung and decaying organic matter. Part of the diverse dung fly community.
Sage leaf miner — tiny agromyzid mining in sage (Salvia) leaves. White serpentine mines on sage leaves diagnostic.
Western long-legged fly — small metallic green dolichopodid. Predatory. Males with elaborate courtship wing-waving on sunlit leaves.
Common thick-legged fly — small hover fly with thickened hind femora. On flowers near water. Larvae in decaying vegetation.
Miller bee fly — medium gray-brown bombyliid dusted with pale scales. Larvae parasitize moth pupae in soil. Common at flowers in foothill habitats.
Beautiful crane fly — small delicate crane fly of moist habitats. Larvae in wet soil.
Franciscan mosquito — medium mosquito. Potential malaria vector (historically). Breeds in freshwater pools. Part of the Anopheles genus of malaria mosquitoes.
Winter marsh mosquito — large mosquito active in cool weather (winter/spring). Breeds in marshes and ditches. Does not vector human disease. Bites livestock.
Beautiful-winged bee fly — medium bee fly with ornate wing patterns. At flowers. Larvae parasitize solitary wasps.
Dark-winged bee fly — medium bee fly with extensively dark wings. Hovering near ground. Parasitoid.
Signed tephritid — small fly with marked wing pattern. On composites. Larvae develop inside flower heads.
California tephritid — small picture-winged fly with ornate wing pattern. On native composites. Part of diverse tephritid fauna.
Mexican fruit fly — medium tephritid. Major quarantine pest — larvae develop inside citrus and mango fruit. Subject of extensive eradication programs in SoCal.
Sagebrush plum gall midge — tiny fly inducing round plum-like galls on sagebrush. Smooth round galls on sage stems.

fm.Color: Tiny; round plum galls on sagebrush

desc: Sacken's robber fly — large bee-mimicking robber fly. Named for dipterist C.R. Osten-Sacken. Powerful aerial predator.

fm.Color: Dense golden pile; bee mimic; large

desc: Emerald lance fly — small metallic lonchaeaid. On sap flows and decaying fruit. Metallic coloring.
Six-spotted hover fly — large hover fly with six spots. Cactus-associated larvae. Bee/wasp mimic.

fm.Color: Dark with six pale spots; bee mimic

desc: Andrews' bee fly — medium bee fly with dark wing patches. Parasitoid of moth pupae. On sandy ground.
Half-black bee fly — medium bee fly with half-dark wings. Larvae parasitize tachinid flies (hyperparasitoid). At flowers.
Willow rose gall midge — tiny fly inducing rosette galls on willow tips resembling small roses. Galls are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Tiny; rose-like galls on willow tips

desc: Interrupted bee fly — small fuzzy bee fly. On sandy soils where hosts nest. Larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees.
Western giant robber fly — very large gray robber fly. One of the largest robber flies in SoCal. Catches large prey in flight including other robber flies.
Insolent bee fly — medium bee fly. At flowers in desert and scrub. Larvae parasitize solitary bees.
Unusual crane fly — large crane fly with distinctive markings. In moist habitats. Harmless despite fearsome appearance.
Vigil flesh fly — medium gray sarcophagid. Larvae can infest wounds on livestock and humans (myiasis). Medically important. Deposits live larvae (not eggs).
Elderberry gall midge — tiny fly inducing flower galls on elderberry (Sambucus). Flowers become swollen and deformed.
Broad-fronted blow fly — medium metallic blue-green blow fly. One of several blow flies in LA. On carrion. Forensically important.
Timberlake's bee fly — small fuzzy bee fly named for entomologist P.H. Timberlake. On sandy soils.
Grooved gall midge — tiny fly inducing grooved/sulcate galls on sagebrush stems.
Similar long-legged fly — medium metallic green dolichopodid. On rocks near streams. Predatory.
Terminal fungus gnat — small dark mycetophilid. Larvae on fungi. Part of the diverse but overlooked fungus gnat community.
Spiny marine midge — remarkable chironomid with FULLY MARINE larvae living in rocky intertidal zone. One of very few marine insects. Adults tiny, short-lived.

fm.Note: Marine intertidal larvae — among few truly marine insects

desc: Yellow-haired sun fly — medium hover fly with skull-like yellow mark on thorax. Common at flowers. Larvae in tree holes and stagnant water.

fm.Color: Dark with skull-like yellow thorax mark

desc: Undivided feather-legged fly — medium tachinid with feathered hind legs. Parasitizes stink bugs. Important biocontrol agent of crop-damaging pentatomids.

fm.Color: Dark; feathered hind legs; parasitizes stink bugs

desc: Panther mydas fly — large mydas fly with spotted pattern. Impressive size. Desert habitats. Bee mimic.
Golden deer fly — medium golden-yellow horse fly with green eyes. Female delivers scissor-like bite. Less aggressive than Chrysops.
Argus mountain black fly — small dark simuliid of mountain streams. Female bites. Larvae filter-feed attached to rocks in fast water. Important stream organism.
Diligent dance fly — small predatory empidid. Males present prey items to females as nuptial gifts during mating — a famous example of sexual selection. In swarms near water.
Yellow-winged mydas fly — very large fly with orange/yellow wings. One of the largest flies. Bee mimic. Impressive.

fm.Color: Dark body; yellow-orange wings; bee mimic

desc: Spotted bee fly — medium bee fly with spotted wings. Larvae parasitize solitary bee and wasp nests. Common at flowers.
Divided bee fly — medium bee fly with divided/split wing pattern. At flowers and hovering near ground.
Agrippina bee fly — medium dark-winged bombyliid. Larvae parasitize moth pupae or ground-nesting insects. Adults at flowers on sandy ground.
Two-spotted soldier fly — small green stratiomyid with two dark spots. Near water. Larvae aquatic.
Graenicher's long-legged fly — small metallic green dolichopodid. Predatory on tiny insects on leaves. Fast runner on foliage.
Green soldier fly — medium metallic green stratiomyid. Common at flowers. Larvae in soil and compost. Attractive metallic coloring.
Mountain soldier fly — tiny dark stratiomyid. Under bark. Larvae in decaying wood. Very small for a soldier fly.
False stable fly — medium gray muscid resembling house fly. In stables, barns, and around livestock. Larvae in decaying organic matter. Can spread disease mechanically.
Cheese skipper — small dark fly breeding in cheese, cured meats, and stored animal products. Larvae can jump several inches ('skipping'). Famous pest of aging cheese.

fm.Behavior: Larvae skip/jump several inches

desc: Similar fruit fly — extremely common fruit fly, nearly identical to D. melanogaster. Major genetics model organism. On rotting fruit worldwide.

fm.Color: Tan; virtually identical to common fruit fly

desc: Red-horned dung fly — tiny sphaerocerid with reddish antennae. On dung and compost.
Ribbed kelp fly — small fly breeding in decaying seaweed on beaches. Very common on wrack lines. Important beach decomposer.
Rayed tephritid — small fly with radiating wing pattern. On composites. Larvae in flower heads.
Dark dance fly — small dark empidid. Predatory. Part of the dance fly family known for male nuptial prey gifts.
Locust gall midge — tiny fly inducing leaf-margin roll galls on black locust (Robinia). Introduced from Europe. Very common on street tree locusts.

fm.Color: Tiny; rolled leaf margins on locust

desc: Variable fungus gnat — small dark fungus gnat. Larvae feed on bracket fungi. At lights near woodland.
Vanderwulp's tachinid — medium tachinid parasitoid. Named for dipterist F.M. van der Wulp.
Three-toothed bee fly — medium bee fly with three-toothed wing cells. Desert and foothill habitats. At flowers.
Banded window gnat — small anisopodid found at windows and on vegetation. Larvae in decaying organic matter.

fm.Color: Dark with pale bands; at windows

desc: Plantain leaf miner — tiny agromyzid mining in plantain (Plantago) leaves. White mines on plantain leaves.
Spotted-face dung fly — tiny sphaerocerid. On decaying organic matter. Part of the detritivore fly guild.
Three-celled bee fly — medium bee fly with distinctive wing venation. On sandy ground. Parasitoid.
Variable bee fly — medium bee fly with variable wing coloring. Parasitoid of various solitary Hymenoptera. Hovers near host burrows.
American acreophthiria — tiny bee fly. Larvae likely parasitize solitary bees. Part of the diverse western bombyliid fauna.
Goldenbush bud gall midge — tiny fly inducing bud galls on goldenbush (Isocoma). Native gall-maker on native shrub.
Painted stiletto fly — medium therevid with colored markings. Desert and foothill sandy habitats.
Yellow tachinid — medium yellow tachinid parasitoid. Attacks stink bug nymphs. Important biocontrol agent.
Narrow eutreta — small tephritid with patterned wings. On various composites. Larvae in stems.
Naked surf fly — tiny fly of rocky intertidal splash zone. Larvae in algae and surf-washed debris. One of the few truly marine flies.
Weak shore fly — tiny ephydrid at water margins. Larvae in aquatic algal mats. Part of diverse shore fly fauna.
Noted gall midge — tiny cecidomyiid inducing galls on various native plants. Each gall species is host-specific. Part of the enormous gall midge family (6,000+ species worldwide).
False creeping leaf miner — tiny agromyzid. Larvae mine in nettle leaves. Part of the diverse leaf-mining fly community.
Zoned bee fly — small beautiful bee fly with golden pile. At flowers. Larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees.
Approximate bot fly — large dark fly whose larvae parasitize rodents. Huge larvae grow under host skin. Adults rarely seen.
Bristly clausticella — small dark tachinid. Parasitoid of caterpillars. Part of the important tachinid parasitoid guild.
Venegasia leaf miner — tiny agromyzid mining canyon sunflower (Venegasia) leaves. Host-specific. Native.
Posterior leaf miner — tiny agromyzid. Larvae create blotch mines in various plant leaves.
Spur robber fly — medium asilid with spurred hind legs. Perches on ground, darting out to catch insects.
Coquillett's robber fly — large hairy robber fly with dense golden pile. Bumblebee mimic. Named for dipterist D.W. Coquillett.
Saltbush blister gall midge — tiny fly inducing blister galls on saltbush (Atriplex) leaves. Common in coastal and desert scrub.
Beautiful-legged blow fly — metallic blue blow fly with distinctive leg ornamentation. On carrion and flowers. Forensically important.
Staircase midge — non-biting chironomid. Larvae aquatic in various freshwater habitats. Important fish food.
Near thick-headed fly — medium conopid parasitizing bees. Female intercepts bees in flight to lay egg. Wasp-like.
Tibial march fly — small dark bibionid swarming in spring. Sluggish swarms. Larvae decompose soil organic matter.
Ventura robber fly — small gray robber fly of sandy habitats. Sits on sand, darting up to catch passing insects.
Ryckman's soldier fly — related to black soldier fly. Larvae decompose organic matter. Native species.
Flat-headed bee fly — medium bee fly with flattened face. Desert habitats. Parasitoid.
Duran's wasp fly — large hover fly mimicking a spider wasp. Dark with metallic sheen. On flowers. Impressive wasp mimic.
Femoral chyromyid — tiny yellowish fly. On vegetation in gardens. Part of the poorly known Chyromyidae.
Single-banded picture-wing fly — small tephritid with single dark wing band. On various plants.

fm.Color: Pale with single dark wing band

desc: Transit bee fly — small bee fly transitioning between hover and perch. On sandy ground.
Ornamented fungus gnat — small dark fungus gnat with ornate thorax markings. In woodland. Larvae on fungi.
Poplar twiggall fly — tiny agromyzid inducing galls on poplar twigs. Galls are swollen twig sections.
Williston's gall midge — tiny cecidomyiid named for dipterist S.W. Williston. Galls on various plants.
Longnose catkin fly — elongated hover fly with long face. Larvae in tree sap runs. On catkins and tree wounds.

fm.Color: Dark; long face; at sap runs

desc: Sticky gall midge — tiny fly inducing sticky galls on sagebrush. Galls exude resinous substance.
Fleabane gall midge — tiny fly inducing galls on fleabane (Erigeron). Bud galls. Host-specific.
Baker's stiletto fly — medium therevid. Larvae predatory in soil. Adults on sandy ground.
Oblique-banded thick-headed fly — medium conopid with angled wing band. Parasitizes bees and wasps internally.

fm.Color: Brown with oblique wing band; parasitizes bees

desc: Sweet crane fly — small crane fly of moist woodland. Larvae in wet soil and moss.
Monkeyflower gall midge — tiny fly inducing galls on monkeyflower (Mimulus/Diplacus).
Johnson's bee fly — medium bee fly named for dipterist C.W. Johnson. On flowers in desert and foothill habitats.
Modest fungus gnat — small dark mycetophilid. Larvae in fungi. Part of woodland litter community.
Dark-legged midge — non-biting chironomid. Larvae aquatic. Part of the dominant freshwater insect community.
Grootaert's big-headed fly — small fly with disproportionately large head. Larvae are internal parasites of leafhoppers. Important biocontrol.
